title: Chapter 1 (expert) — Deterministic loop & numerical integration
meta.desc: Expert level: fixed timestep and authoritative server, semi-implicit Euler integration and its error, and determinism through a seeded RNG so that server and clients see the same match.
hero.title: Deterministic loop & numerical integration
hero.lead: Deep in the engine: why a fixed timestep and an authoritative server, what Euler integration is really worth, and how to guarantee that one and the same calculation always produces the same match.
s1.lead: The simulation does not advance at the pace of the display. The server steps it in fixed steps of 33 ms (≈ 30 Hz), regardless of each screen's fps.
s1.p1: A constant step has two virtues. First, numerical stability: the integration error no longer depends on an erratic dt (a lag spike no longer “teleports” a ball through a tower). Second, determinism: same inputs + same steps ⇒ same result, bit for bit.
s1.callout: Authority. The clients never integrate the physics. They send intents (angle, power, “ready”) and only display the snapshots they get back. This eliminates disagreements between machines — and cheating via a modified client.
s2.p1: The trajectory obeys a differential equation (constant acceleration per step). We approximate it with the simplest method, Euler integration — but in its semi-implicit (symplectic) variant: we update the velocity first, then the position using that fresh velocity.
s2.p2: “Naive” Euler (position before velocity) drifts and inflates energy over time; the semi-implicit variant stays far more stable for the same cost. The local error is O(dt²): at 30 Hz, for flights of a few seconds, the gap is invisible — and, crucially, it is the same for everyone.
s2.callout: Sub-steps. Need more precision without speeding up the clock? Split each tick into N sub-steps of dt/N. The shot simulator in the discovery chapter already does this (2 sub-steps per frame) to smooth the curve. It's the classic precision/cost trade-off.
s3.h2: Reproducibility: the seed is the truth
s3.p1: For the server and every client to draw exactly the same terrain, we banish Math.random() from everything that has to be shared. Instead, a seeded pseudo-random generator: same seed ⇒ same sequence of numbers ⇒ same world.
s3.code.cap: the terrain is a pure function of the seed
s3.code.c1: // the server sends only the seed; each client regenerates it identically
s3.p2: The snapshot therefore carries the seed, not the hundreds of terrain heights — bandwidth saving and a consistency guarantee. Same principle for the hidden jitter of shots: drawn from the server's RNG, never transmitted, so invisible and yet reproducible on the server side.
s3.rng.cap: src/sim/rng.js — mulberry32, a PRNG with a tiny seed
s3.callout: Remember. Fixed-step loop + server authority + seeded RNG = a deterministic, replayable match. It's the invisible skeleton that makes everything else possible: networking, destructible terrain, continuous wind.
